quiz.title: Grade-10 Physics: Unit 2 Quiz 12
quiz.subtitle: Force Calculations, Friction, and Newton's Laws
quiz.subject: Physics

questions.question:
A wooden block is being pulled across a floor at a constant speed by a force of $10\text{ N}$. A second, identical wooden block is then stacked directly on top of the first one. The person pulling the blocks intends to keep them moving at the same constant speed as before.

Assuming the surface conditions remain the same, how much force must the person now apply to the two-block stack?
questions.explanation: Doubling the mass doubles the normal force, which doubles the kinetic friction. Since constant speed requires applied force to equal friction, the force must double from $10\text{ N}$ to $20\text{ N}$.

questions.question:
A puck is sliding across a sheet of ice that has been treated to be perfectly frictionless. The puck is moving to the right at a steady speed of $8\text{ m/s}$. A scientist observes the puck as it crosses a specific marker on the floor. At that exact moment, there are no horizontal forces—neither a push nor a pull—acting on the puck.

Which of the following describes the motion of the puck five seconds after it passes the marker?
options: The puck will have come to a complete stop
The puck will be moving to the right at exactly $8\text{ m/s}$
The puck will be moving to the right at a speed greater than $8\text{ m/s}$
The puck will have begun moving backward toward the marker
questions.explanation: With no net force acting on it, the puck continues at constant velocity (Newton's first law). It maintains $8\text{ m/s}$ to the right.

questions.question:
Two students are watching a large, heavy truck collide with a small, lightweight parked car. Both vehicles are made of the same materials. After the collision, the small car is pushed back several meters, while the truck barely slows down. The students debate the forces involved during the moment of impact.

Student A: "The truck must have exerted a much larger force on the car than the car exerted on the truck, which is why the car flew backward."

Student B: "The forces were actually equal, but the car reacted more violently because it has much less mass than the truck."

Which student is correct, and why?
options: Student A, because the larger object always wins a force battle against a smaller object
Student B, because Newton's third law is obeyed
Student A, because Newton's second law is obeyed
Student B, because the car's engine was off, meaning it couldn't exert any force at all
questions.explanation: Newton's third law states the forces are equal and opposite. The car accelerates more because it has less mass ($a = F/m$), not because the force on it was larger.

questions.question:
What is the 'apparent weight' of the student recorded by the scale during the initial upward acceleration?

"A student stands on a calibrated spring scale inside a high-speed elevator in a skyscraper. On the ground floor, while the elevator is at rest, the scale stays constant at a reading of $540\text{ N}$. As the elevator begins its ascent, it accelerates upward at a steady rate of $1.5\text{ m/s}^2$. The student notices that during this brief period of acceleration, the reading on the scale changes significantly before returning to the original value once the elevator reaches a constant cruising speed."
questions.explanation: Mass $m = 540 / 9.8 \approx 55.1\text{ kg}$. $F_{net} = ma = 55.1 \times 1.5 \approx 82.7\text{ N}$. Apparent weight $F_N = F_g + F_{net} = 540 + 82.7 \approx 622.5\text{ N}$.

questions.question:
What is the magnitude of the upward force exerted by the elevator floor on the crate during this period of deceleration?

"A technician is transporting a delicate $22\text{ kg}$ equipment crate in a service elevator. As the elevator nears the top floor, it begins to slow down from its cruising speed, decelerating at a rate of $1.8\text{ m/s}^2$. The crate is resting on a pressure-sensitive platform that measures the upward contact force exerted by the elevator floor. The technician notes that the 'weight' displayed on the platform's digital readout drops the moment the deceleration begins."
questions.explanation: $F_g = 22 \times 9.8 = 215.6\text{ N}$. $F_{net} = 22 \times 1.8 = 39.6\text{ N}$. Moving up but slowing down means acceleration is downward, so $F_N = F_g - F_{net} = 215.6 - 39.6 = 176.0\text{ N}$.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements correctly explains the forces acting on Sphere B during the final $200\text{ m}$ of its fall?

"In a physics simulation, two identical $1.5\text{ kg}$ spheres are dropped from a height of $500\text{ m}$. Sphere A is dropped in a total vacuum where air has been completely removed. Sphere B is dropped in a standard atmosphere. The simulation tracks their velocity over time. The researcher observes that while Sphere A continues to speed up at a constant rate until it impacts the ground, Sphere B reaches a point where its velocity levels off and remains constant for the final $200\text{ m}$ of its fall."
options: The force of gravity has been neutralized by the height, resulting in zero weight
The upward force of air resistance is equal in magnitude to the downward force of gravity
The sphere has run out of kinetic energy, preventing further acceleration
The vacuum of space is pulling upward on the sphere with a force of $14.7\text{ N}$
questions.explanation: Sphere B has reached terminal velocity. At this point, air resistance (upward) equals gravity (downward), so net force is zero and velocity remains constant.

questions.question:
An object is being acted upon by three constant forces:

A $12\text{ N}$ force pulling it to the North.
A $12\text{ N}$ force pulling it to the South.
A $5\text{ N}$ force pulling it to the East.

Given the forces described above, which of the following states of motion is physically impossible for this object?
options: The object is moving to the East with a constant acceleration
The object is moving to the East with a constant deceleration
The object is moving to the North at zero velocity
The object was initially at rest but is now starting to move East
questions.explanation: The North-South forces cancel, leaving a net $5\text{ N}$ East. This means constant acceleration East. Deceleration East is impossible because the net force acts East, not West.

questions.question:
Based on the laboratory data provided, what is the coefficient of kinetic friction ($\mu_k$) between the plastic block and the steel workbench?

"A student is performing a materials test in a physics lab. She uses a force plate to pull a $3.5\text{ kg}$ block of textured plastic across a steel workbench at a constant velocity. The force plate shows that a steady horizontal pull of $8.4\text{ N}$ is required to keep the block moving. The student knows that since the velocity is constant, the applied force must be exactly equal to the force of kinetic friction."

What is the minimum horizontal force the robot must exert to get the crate to start moving?

"A maintenance robot is attempting to push a heavy crate across a warehouse floor. The crate has a total mass of $62\text{ kg}$. The coefficient of static friction ($\mu_s$) between the crate and the floor is $0.35$. The robot is programmed to increase its pushing force gradually until the crate begins to slide. To initiate movement, the robot must overcome the maximum static friction of the contact surface."
questions.explanation: $F_N = 62 \times 9.8 = 607.6\text{ N}$. Maximum static friction $= \mu_s \times F_N = 0.35 \times 607.6 \approx 212.7\text{ N}$.

questions.question:
What is the magnitude of the kinetic friction force acting on the aluminum slider?

"In a precision micro-mechanics experiment, a small aluminum slider with a mass of $340\text{ g}$ is placed on a polished track. The coefficient of kinetic friction for the slider on this track is $0.22$. A technician needs to calculate the resistive force acting on the slider to calibrate the drive motor for a smooth, constant-speed run."
questions.explanation: Convert $340\text{ g}$ to $0.34\text{ kg}$. $F_N = 0.34 \times 9.8 = 3.332\text{ N}$. $F_f = \mu_k \times F_N = 0.22 \times 3.332 \approx 0.73\text{ N}$.

scoreMessages.90: Excellent! You've mastered force calculations, friction coefficients, and Newton's laws!
scoreMessages.70: Good work! Review Newton's third law and the relationship between friction and normal force.
scoreMessages.50: Keep practicing! Focus on calculating normal force ($F_N = mg$) and using $F_f = \mu F_N$.
scoreMessages.0: Review the basics: Newton's laws, friction formulas, and unit conversions (grams to kilograms).
